what_it_does: Installs a global window.fetch interceptor and mints a per-session operator token via GET /api/operator-session, attaching it as the X-Operator-Session header to same-origin /api requests so server-side validation can gate consequential operations while reads remain ungated.
observations: The token is minted from GET /api/operator-session and stored in a module-level let.
A single global fetch wrapper is installed once and merges the X-Operator-Session header into same-origin /api calls.
Cross-origin requests are explicitly excluded from header attachment.
On 404 or any mint error the token stays null and the interceptor adds nothing, leaving behavior unchanged.
The client never sends a supervised/autonomous privilege flag; it only carries the server-minted token.
EventSource/SSE streams are not intercepted because they are reads.
The wrapper preserves existing headers and init, rebuilding Request inputs when necessary.
standouts: Transparent, server-minted token attached automatically to all same-origin /api fetches.
Clean degradation when the mint endpoint is absent: zero behavior change until enforcement is flipped.
Explicit refusal to assert privilege client-side; all privilege decisions are left to server-side session context.
conventions: Uses module-level mutable state for the token and an installed guard flag.
Wraps global fetch once rather than threading auth through every call site.
Uses the Headers API to merge headers without clobbering existing ones.
Fire-and-forget async mint during app initialization.
concerns: Global fetch mutation is irreversible and may conflict with other interceptors.
Token is held only in memory with no visible expiry, refresh, or revocation logic.
No retry or backoff if the mint fails; the token remains null until a page reload.
Rebuilding a Request input may drop or alter some Request init properties.
Mint response shape is parsed loosely without a defined TypeScript type.
notes: This is the surface-side half of a transparent, server-validated operator session: it auto-mints a token and silently attaches it to same-origin API calls.
questions: What is the token format and how does the server validate it?
Is there a refresh or revocation path when the token expires or the session ends?
How does the server distinguish supervised versus autonomous sessions if the client sends the same token?
Could another fetch wrapper in the app double-wrap or conflict with this interceptor?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose of transparently minting and attaching a per-session operator token for server-side gating matches the implementation.
apparent_themes: Transparent security with no visible auth step for the operator.
Server-side authority: the client carries a token but never asserts privilege.
Safe incremental rollout via clean degradation before enforcement.
Minimal-impact interception: reads and cross-origin traffic are unaffected.
intention_signals: It is trying to provide a spoof-resistant, background-agent-blocking authorization signal for high-stakes API actions without changing the operator's visible flow or gating routine reads.
novelty: The design treats the client as a passive token carrier and deliberately avoids any client-side privilege flag, relying entirely on server-side session context for supervised-vs-autonomous decisions.
contribution: A reusable, global fetch-interceptor pattern for attaching a server-minted session token to same-origin API calls, with explicit safe-fallback behavior when the mint endpoint is unavailable.
summary_for_embedding: A TypeScript module that installs a global fetch interceptor to mint and attach an X-Operator-Session token to same-origin /api requests for server-validated consequential operations.
intention_for_embedding: To enable transparent, server-side authorization of high-stakes API actions by auto-provisioning a per-session operator token on the surface.

symbols.does: Determines whether a request URL targets the same-origin /api path.
symbols.description: Returns true for relative /api/ URLs or absolute URLs whose origin and pathname match the current window's origin and /api prefix; used to limit token attachment to the app's own API surface and prevent cross-origin leakage.

symbols.does: Fetches a per-session operator token from the server.
symbols.description: Calls GET /api/operator-session and, on a successful response, extracts a token from common response fields. On any non-ok response or error it leaves the token null so the interceptor attaches nothing and pre-enforcement requests continue to work.

symbols.does: Exposes the currently held operator session token.
symbols.description: Simple accessor returning the module-level token string or null; useful for callers that need to inspect whether a token has been minted.

symbols.does: Wraps window.fetch once to inject the X-Operator-Session header.
symbols.description: Installs a single global fetch interceptor that merges the operator token into request headers for same-origin /api calls while preserving existing headers and init; it is idempotent via the installed flag and falls back to the original fetch if anything goes wrong.
Calls installOperatorSessionFetch and then fires mintOperatorSession asynchronously so the interceptor begins attaching the token as soon as it is available.
